title: PySpark Test Track
description: Test track with 3 sample modules: Junior DataFrames, Mid-Level Join Performance, Senior Partition Strategy.

chapters.title: DataFrames — The Core Abstraction
chapters.description: Understand DataFrames as Spark's primary distributed data structure, master creation and schema patterns, learn the immutability and lazy evaluation models.

blocks.content: Spark does not execute your transformations when you write them. It records them into a plan — a sequence of steps that describe what you want but do not actually process any data. Only when you call an action — show(), count(), write(), collect() — does Spark look at the entire plan, optimize it, and execute it. This behavior is called lazy evaluation, and it is the mechanism that makes Spark fast.
Building the Plan

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, year

# Step 1-5: Define transformations — no data is read yet
df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
df = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
df = df.filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')
df = df.withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
df = df.select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'year')

# ZERO data has been read from disk.
# THIS triggers execution:
df.show(5)
What Catalyst Optimizes

Catalyst combines the two filters into one, pushes the region filter into the Parquet reader as a partition filter (skipping 75% of data at filesystem level), and applies projection pruning to read only 3 columns. You wrote five steps; Spark executes two.

blocks.title: Your code vs what Catalyst actually executes
blocks.content:
Your Code (5 steps)                    Catalyst's Execution
────────────────────────                    ───────────────────────
1. read all columns, all partitions     Read parquet:
2. filter daily_kw > 0          ─►       • Partition filter: region=NORTH
3. filter region == 'NORTH'              • Column pruning: 3 cols only
4. withColumn year(date)                 • Pushed filter: daily_kw > 0
5. select 3 columns                    Compute year(date), output

Naive: read 2GB                         Optimized: read ~250MB (8x less)
blocks.caption: Lazy evaluation lets Catalyst see all five steps before executing any of them.

blocks.content: Seeing the Plan with explain()
df.explain(True)  # Shows plan WITHOUT executing

== Optimized Logical Plan ==
Project [meter_id#0, daily_kw#1, year(date#2) AS year#5]
+- Filter (isnotnull(daily_kw#1) AND (daily_kw#1 > 0.0))
   +- FileScan parquet [meter_id#0, daily_kw#1, date#2]
      PartitionFilters: [region#3 = NORTH]
      PushedFilters: [IsNotNull(daily_kw), GreaterThan(daily_kw, 0.0)]

The plan reveals every optimization: PartitionFilters skips non-NORTH folders, PushedFilters applies during scan, and ReadSchema shows only 3 columns read. Catalyst even added IsNotNull automatically since null > 0 is always null.
Transformations vs Actions
The Cost of Unnecessary Actions

# DEVELOPMENT — intermediate actions OK for debugging
df = spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
df = df.filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
print(f"After filter: {df.count()} rows")  # reads file from scratch
df = df.filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')
df.show(5)  # reads file from scratch AGAIN
# File read TWICE

# PRODUCTION — one action at the end
df = (spark.read.parquet('bronze/meters/')
    .filter(col('daily_kw') > 0)
    .filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')
    .withColumn('year', year(col('date')))
    .select('meter_id', 'daily_kw', 'year'))
df.write.parquet('silver/meters_north/')  # file read ONCE

blocks.title: Common mistake
blocks.content: Calling count() inside a loop or after every transformation in production. Each count() triggers a full execution from scratch. Use show(5) during development (cheap — stops after 5 rows). In production, chain transformations and trigger one action (write) at the end.

chapters.title: Join Performance and Shuffle Optimization
chapters.description: Master the performance characteristics of distributed joins — broadcast strategies, shuffle mechanics, skew detection, and optimizer behaviors.

blocks.content: NordGrid's daily meter pipeline ran reliably for six months at 22 minutes. Then it started taking 3 hours. Nothing in the code changed. The Spark UI showed 19 of 20 tasks completed in under 2 minutes, but one task ran for 2 hours 48 minutes. NORTH region grew from 25% to 65% of all readings. The shuffle sent 65% of data to one executor. This is data skew.
What Skew Looks Like

blocks.title: Skewed shuffle — one executor overwhelmed
blocks.content:
Executor 1 (NORTH)     Executor 2 (SOUTH)     Executor 3 (EAST)      Executor 4 (WEST)
6,500,000 rows          1,800,000 rows          1,200,000 rows         500,000 rows
Takes 6.5 min           Takes 1.8 min           Takes 1.2 min          Takes 0.5 min

Executors 2-4 idle for 4-6 minutes waiting for Executor 1
Job time: 6.5 min (determined by slowest executor)
Effective utilization: 40%
blocks.caption: Skew turns a parallel job into a sequential bottleneck.

blocks.content: Strategy 1: Salting
Salting splits a hot key into multiple sub-keys. Add a random suffix so NORTH rows distribute across 10 partitions (NORTH_0 through NORTH_9). Expand the small side to match all salt values.

from pyspark.sql.functions import col, concat, lit, floor, rand, explode, array

SALT_BUCKETS = 10

# Add random salt to the LARGE side
readings_salted = readings.withColumn(
    'salt', floor(rand() * SALT_BUCKETS).cast('int')
).withColumn(
    'region_salted', concat(col('region'), lit('_'), col('salt'))
)

# Explode the SMALL side to match all salt values
metadata_exploded = metadata.withColumn(
    'salt', explode(array([lit(i) for i in range(SALT_BUCKETS)]))
).withColumn(
    'region_salted', concat(col('region'), lit('_'), col('salt'))
)

# Join on salted key
result = readings_salted.join(metadata_exploded, 'region_salted', 'inner')
    .drop('salt', 'region_salted')

rows.left: NORTH partition: 6.5M rows on one executor
rows.right: NORTH split into 10 partitions of ~650K each

blocks.content: Strategy 2: AQE Skew Handling

# Enable AQE — no code changes to the join itself
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled', 'true')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor', '5')
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes', '256m')

# Normal join — AQE detects and splits skewed partitions automatically
result = readings.join(metadata, 'region', 'inner')
Choosing Between Strategies

blocks.title: Production pattern
blocks.content: Enable AQE as baseline defense for moderate skew. For extreme skew in annual reprocessing, add manual salting with 20 buckets. Monitor max/median task duration ratio weekly — if it exceeds 10x, investigate.

chapters.title: Partition Strategy at Scale
chapters.description: Design, implement, and maintain partition strategies for multi-terabyte datasets.

blocks.content: GridUnion's Silver meter readings table was partitioned by date only. It holds 8TB spanning 3 years. Twelve downstream pipelines and 40 Power BI reports depend on it. After acquiring three subsidiaries, the EU regulator requires country-level data isolation — the partition scheme must change to country/date within 90 days.
The Three Migration Approaches
Approach 1: Dual-Write Migration

Create a new table alongside the old one. Modify the write pipeline to write to both simultaneously. Migrate consumers one by one. Once all switched, backfill historical data, decommission the old table. Zero downtime, but 2x storage and write cost during migration (2-6 weeks).

def write_daily_batch_dual(batch_df, batch_date):
    batch_df = batch_df.withColumn('country',
        when(col('region').startswith('DE-'), lit('DE'))
        .when(col('region').startswith('FR-'), lit('FR'))
        .when(col('region').startswith('NL-'), lit('NL'))
        .otherwise(lit('UNKNOWN'))
    )
    batch_df.cache()
    batch_df.count()  # materialize cache
    
    # Write to BOTH tables
    batch_df.write.mode('append').partitionBy('date').parquet('silver/meter_readings/')
    batch_df.write.mode('append').partitionBy('country', 'date').parquet('silver/meter_readings_v2/')
    batch_df.unpersist()
Approach 2: View Abstraction

Create a UNION ALL view combining new data (new partition scheme) and historical data (old scheme). Consumers read the view transparently. Gradually backfill historical data. Requires all consumers to use catalog names, not direct paths.
Approach 3: Delta Lake CRAS

CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE AS SELECT rewrites the entire 8TB table atomically. Delta's MVCC ensures readers see the pre-migration snapshot until the commit. One command, 4-8 hours, zero consumer code changes.

spark.sql("""
    CREATE OR REPLACE TABLE silver.meter_readings
    USING DELTA
    PARTITIONED BY (country, date)
    AS SELECT
        meter_id, daily_kw, date, region,
        CASE WHEN region LIKE 'DE-%' THEN 'DE'
             WHEN region LIKE 'FR-%' THEN 'FR'
             WHEN region LIKE 'NL-%' THEN 'NL'
             ELSE 'UNKNOWN'
        END AS country,
        customer_type, substation_id
    FROM silver.meter_readings
""")

-- After: OPTIMIZE + VACUUM
spark.sql("OPTIMIZE silver.meter_readings ZORDER BY (meter_id)")
spark.sql("VACUUM silver.meter_readings RETAIN 168 HOURS")
Decision Framework
Operational Playbook

items: Pre-migration audit (Week 1): Inventory consumers, document current scheme, model target scheme, get budget approval for storage.
Communication (Week 2): Notify consumer owners, schedule maintenance window, alert on-call rotation.
Dry run (Week 3): Execute in staging on 1TB sample. Verify downstream outputs match. Measure duration.
Production execution (Week 4): Execute during maintenance window. Monitor Spark UI. Validate row counts and checksums.
Post-migration validation (Week 4-5): Run all downstream pipelines. Compare outputs. Confirm regulatory compliance. Monitor for one week.
Cleanup (Week 6): VACUUM old files. Remove migration code. Update documentation.

blocks.title: Architectural principle
blocks.content: The best repartitioning is the one you never need. Invest 30 minutes in design review during initial table creation. Model partition sizes for 2-3 year volumes. A 30-minute review prevents a 6-week migration project.
